title: Back From the Dead: A UX Story
excerpt: The death toll in our industry is unlike anything I have seen before. Figma is dead. Design is dead. UX is dead. At this point I am surprised anyone showed up to work this morning.
content:
The death toll in our industry is unlike anything I have seen before. Figma is dead. Design is dead. UX is dead. Vibe coding killed the developer. AI killed the designer. At this point I am surprised anyone showed up to work this morning. Someone discovers a new AI tool, spins up a screen in four minutes, and declares that designers are pushing up daisies. Everyone is so eager to be the person who declared DOA that they skip right past the part where they ask whether the thing they built is actually any good.

The Eulogy Nobody Asked For

Google Stitch dropped an update and my feed lit up with eulogies for an industry that is very much still alive. Vibe coding became a thing and suddenly everyone with a laptop and a Claude subscription is a product designer. I get the excitement. I genuinely do. What these tools can do is remarkable and I am not being sarcastic when I say that. The creative and technical capability AI has put in people's hands is something I have not felt in this industry since the Flash era, and this is going to be much bigger.

Camera Tech Didn't Kill Photographers

But there is a difference between believing in a tool and believing that the tool eliminates the need for expertise. Nobody looks at a high-end camera and concludes that photographers are obsolete. The camera got better. The eye behind it still matters.

So if you came here looking for a designer shaking his fist at the sky, you are in the wrong place. I think people are going to keep building interfaces on their own and exploring what these tools can do, and some of them are going to land on genuinely great ideas. You never know where inspiration comes from and I think that kind of democratization is worth celebrating.

Not So Fast

What I am not willing to celebrate is the idea that user experience no longer matters. Because that is just not true, and the market has a long and consistent history of reminding people of that fact in the most expensive way possible.

Built Is Not the Same as Designed

Building something and designing something are not the same thing. They have never been the same thing. UX is not a coat of paint you apply at the end or a Figma file you hand to a developer. It is the thinking that happens before a single component gets placed on a screen. It is understanding how people move through a task, where they get confused, what they expect to happen next, and how to make the whole thing feel so effortless that they never have to think about it. That work does not disappear because AI can generate a layout. If anything it matters more now, because the volume of things being built is about to increase dramatically and most of them are going to feel exactly like what they are: interfaces nobody thought deeply about.

Exhibit A

Talking about UX is a little like talking about music. At some point you just have to press play. Fair warning before you do though, one of these is going to age you visibly.
